title: How to Score 300+ in JAMB UTME: A Complete Strategy Guide
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Scoring above 300 in the Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination is the goal for thousands of candidates each year. A score in that range opens doors to competitive courses like Medicine, Law, and Engineering at top universities. But the UTME is not just a test of knowledge. It is a test of strategy. The candidates who score 300 and above are not necessarily the ones who studied the most hours. They are the ones who understood how the exam works and prepared accordingly. Here is the strategic approach to beating the UTME.

Understand How the Exam Is Scored

The UTME consists of four subjects. English Language is compulsory for all candidates. The remaining three subjects depend on your intended course of study. Each subject is tested through forty multiple-choice questions for a total of one hundred sixty questions across the four subjects. Each question carries roughly 2.5 marks, for a total possible score of 400.

The exam is computer-based and time-limited. You have two hours to answer all one hundred sixty questions. That gives you roughly forty-five seconds per question. This is not generous. You cannot afford to spend two minutes wrestling with a difficult question. Speed and accuracy must be balanced. The candidates who score highest are not necessarily the ones who know the most. They are the ones who know when to answer and when to move on.

There is no penalty for wrong answers in the UTME. A guessed answer has a twenty-five percent chance of being correct. A blank answer has zero percent. Never leave a question unanswered. If you are running out of time, fill in every remaining question with your best guess. The expected value of guessing is positive. Use it.

Choose Your Subject Combination Strategically

Your subject combination is not just about meeting the requirements for your intended course. It is about maximizing your total score. Some subjects are more scoring than others. By this, I do not mean they are easier. I mean that with equivalent preparation, candidates tend to score higher in some subjects than in others. This varies by individual, but there are patterns.

If you have a choice between subjects, consider which ones play to your strengths. A candidate who is strong in calculations and logical reasoning may score higher in Mathematics or Physics than in Government or Literature. A candidate who is strong in reading and writing may score higher in Government or CRS than in Chemistry. Your subject combination should satisfy your course requirements, but within those requirements, choose the subjects where you can maximize your score. The UTME does not care which three subjects you took. It only cares about the total across all four.

Prioritize English Language in Your Preparation

English Language is the one subject every candidate must take, and it is where many candidates lose marks unnecessarily. The English paper tests comprehension, vocabulary, grammar, and oral English. The comprehension section requires you to read passages and answer questions about them. The lexis and structure section tests your vocabulary and grammatical accuracy. The oral English section tests your knowledge of pronunciation, stress patterns, and intonation.

English is also a subject where small improvements compound. Learning fifty new vocabulary words might earn you three or four additional marks. Mastering the rules for question tags might earn you two more. These small gains add up. A candidate who scores 65 in English and 75 in their other three subjects finishes with 290. The same candidate who pushes English to 75 finishes with 300. The ten-mark difference in English is the difference between a good score and a great one. Do not neglect English. It is worth the same number of marks as every other subject.

Master the CBT Interface Before Exam Day

The UTME is computer-based, and technical unfamiliarity costs candidates marks every year. The interface is simple, but it is different from paper-based exams. You select answers by clicking. You navigate between questions using on-screen buttons or keyboard shortcuts. You can flag questions to return to later. You can see at a glance which questions you have answered and which you have not.

Practice with CBT simulation software before the exam. Several free and paid options are available. The goal is to make the interface so familiar that you do not think about it during the actual exam. Your cognitive resources should be spent on the questions, not on figuring out how to use the software. A candidate who is comfortable with the CBT interface answers questions faster and with less anxiety than one who is not.

Use the keyboard shortcuts. The most important shortcut is using the number keys or the A, B, C, D keys to select answers. This is faster than clicking with the mouse. Every second saved on navigation is a second gained for thinking. Over the course of one hundred sixty questions, the time saved by using keyboard shortcuts can be the difference between finishing comfortably and rushing through the final questions.

Develop a Question-Triage System

You will encounter questions of varying difficulty on the UTME. Some will be straightforward. You will know the answer immediately. Some will require thought. You will need to reason through them. Some will be difficult or unfamiliar. You may not know how to approach them. Your job is to triage these questions in real time.

Answer the easy questions first. They secure marks quickly and build confidence. For the medium-difficulty questions, give yourself up to a minute. If you have not arrived at an answer by then, make your best guess, flag the question, and move on. For the difficult questions, guess immediately, flag, and move on. Do not spend three minutes on a single question. The time you save by moving on is time you can invest in questions you are more likely to answer correctly.

If you finish the section with time remaining, return to your flagged questions. Your brain will have processed the question in the background, and you may see it more clearly on the second pass. But never sacrifice easy marks later in the section for the chance of earning a difficult mark earlier. The easy question is worth the same as the difficult one.

The Bottom Line

Scoring 300 or more in the UTME requires a strategic approach, not just hard work. Understand the scoring system and the time constraints. Choose your subject combination to maximize your total score within your course requirements. Prioritize English Language as a source of incremental gains. Master the CBT interface through simulation practice. Develop a question-triage system that secures easy marks quickly and prevents difficult questions from consuming your time. The candidates who score highest are not necessarily the ones who studied the most. They are the ones who combined knowledge with strategy and executed on exam day.
